1 JOHN TAAFFE. -

In Mr. O’Donoghue’s wonderful work ‘‘ The Poets of Ireland,” the
name of John Taaffe is recorded, and beyond the fact that he published l
in 1816 a poem entitled ‘‘ Padilla,” the learned compiler confesses | r
that he was unable to glean any details concerning his career. It is TERR
too interesting to be undeserving of a mnotice in I.B.L.

He was the eldest son by his second marriage of John Taaffe, of . 1
Smarmore Castle, in the County of Louth. His mother’s maiden name ;
~ # was Catherine Taaffe, and although she belonged to a different branch
of the family, her son could claim that through hoth of his parents |
‘1  the best blood of one of the noblest families of the County in which he

* was born coursed through his veins. He was born in 1787, the record _- “

: of his baptism is preserved under date 6th IFebruary. In the first 4
4 decade of the 19th century he found himself in Edinburgh, whitner he ,f
' went, we are told, to make the acquaintance of Jeffrey, of the “Edin- '
% ; burgh Review,” and Sir Walter Scott, and where he attended the v Ei) 4‘
| lectures of Dugald Stewart and the famous chemist Hope. He was : 1,;
‘““a gentlemanly hittle man—a very pretty gentleman ’’—and he had ‘ ,;;]
*"' the reputation. in that city of being the heir to a fine "castle at Smar- | i
| more and five thousand aj year. He won the attention of a handsomo ‘%
" I young widow, a Mrs. Colebrooke, for whom a future Lord Mountgarrot m
ﬁf f- had also conceived a strong affection. He decamped from Td inhurga e E
by a smack to Berwick, and he married the lady at Preston. The per- : g
l;f 4 sonal debts of the whilom widow were nob, it appears, inconsiderable, -l -‘i
and Taaffe’s father promptly out b im off from certain hopes of dis-

to his terror on awakening one da..y from a siesta upon his sofa, founq
his witeé approaching him temerariously with an open razor in her
fingers. She died soon afterwards, insane.

Taaffe took to travelling and now and again to writing poetry. Hig
poetry is poor stuff, but the notes and comments which he appendeqd
to his verses are both scholarly and interesting. They contain a great
number of delightful personal allusions. Irom the notes to ** Padilla”
we learn that previously to 1816 he had travelled much 1n Portugal.
He tells of incidents that occurred to him at Oporto. He relates with
detail a visit he paid to the tomb of Egaz Moniz at the monastery of
Paco de Sousa ‘‘ about seven leagues from Oporto.”” How on his re-
turn he was threatened by a vicious wolf. How at another time he
visited all that remained of the monastery at Alcobago, and saw the
famous library there, whose property ‘‘in printed volumes is very im-
mense, and whose manuscripts form, after the Torre de Tombo, the
most ancient and numerous collection in the Kingdom.”
~ In his notes to the ‘“ Comment on Dante,”” we find stories of Oporto
and of the savagery he witnessed amongst the Moors in Ta.ngiea's.. On
Christmas Day in 1812 we find him at a splendid feast given by Lord
Byron in what was called his Pistol Cluly at Pisa. He is there amongsk
such a distinguished company as Byron, Shelley, Medwin and Tre-
lawney. Byron showed himself particularly friendly towards him, and
although he laughed at his poetry as was becoming, he valued him
as a scholar and a friend. He accompanied Byron daily upon his rides
from Pisa, and he joined him very frequently in his favourite sport of
pistol firing. At this time Taaffe had made a profound study of all
the works of Dante, and particularly of his famous one. He was conur
versant with the English translaifion which was the first full traduction

into that language of the poet’s masterpiece, and which b y the way

was made by an Irishman. He was dissatisfied with this translation,
ﬂ.pd' he essayed a versified translation of the *‘Inferno? himself.
Ifrom the disjointed specimens of his verse which are given 1n his
comment we may well imagine how Byron laughed. But he admired
it for its scholarship, and he induced, rather compelled, Murray t0
publish one volume, which appeared anonymously in 1822 under the
title, ‘‘ A Comment on Dante.”” This book displays uncommon T¢-
search, and it is a fine tribute to Taaffe’s love for hooks and recondito
literary lore. 'Taaffe was an awkward horseman, and he seems un

gittingly to have been the occasion of the fracas that compelled Byron
+o evacute Pisa. He married a rich Italian lady of high birth, and ne
pecame a Kuight of the Order of St. John of Jerusalem. He was
devoted to the Order, and he wrote a history of its achievements which
was published in 1852 in four volumes by Hope & Co., London. In his
old age, at Fano, in Italy, he was waited upon by a commission of
lawyers from Ireland to relieve his old rival Butler from the imputa-
tion of having married in Edinburgh that handsome widow Colebrooke,
whom he married himself to his grief at Preston. He died at Fano in
1862 at the ripe age of 85.
THOMAS GOGARTY.
ULSTER IN SONG.

Dr. Sigerson, President of the National Literary Society, delivered
the inaugural lecture of the twenty-first session, in the Hall, 6, St.
Stephen’s Green, Dublin. Count Plunkett presided, and the Ilarge
attendance included his Excellency the Lord Lieutenant, the Countess
of Aberdeen and panty.

Dr. Sigerson said he spoke of a land of legend, song, and high
romance. Its ethereal realm had bounds in the unseen world of the
ever-present past. Still in the East it heard the waters of Moyle and
the swans of Lir ; still in the West it saw in happy vision the halcyon
isles of youth and joy. Beneath its great grey lake lay the submerged
city of ancient kings, and in the hosom of its royal hill sleeping war-
tiors awaited the auspicious dawn. Still, when the three waves of
Erinn which defend her, rise and reverberate around her shores, the
voice of the Wave of Cliona in the South joined in harmonious unison
with the voices of the Waves of Ruri and Aevil, thunderin g from the
Arctic Pole. Then from its deep sea palace, close to the columnar
crags of mythic giants, Manannan, the oceam lord arose, and led the
fairy cavalcade over the land he loved. Was it wonderful that in that
énchanted clime the spirit and rhythmic form of poetry appeared eaxrly
and 'lourished, like flowers under happy skies. It was marvellous that
there arose, so soon and so suddenly, costumed and crowned in full

majesty, the greatest epic cycle that Irveland possessed—one that by:

ity Jofty ideals and supreme imagination, though not by form, claimed

& foremost place amongst the epics of the world. That was the:
Cuchulainn cydle, where they saw, sinuously interlacing, like serpents. .

around the Laocoon group, the monster vices surrounding the mighty
virtues. Honour, Love and Chivalry in the toils of Jealousy, Treachery,
and Revenge, incarnated in actual human life, so perfect and so com-
plete that they forgot the skill of the conception; they moved in a
tumult of heroes, and were impelled to share in theiwr strenuous strife.
There was the epic of Deirdre, the first of the Sorrows of Story. The
idealll of nationhood was contained therein.

Passing to a later spoch, he referred to Saint Columba, born in the
region now called Donegal.  His poems were in Latin and Gaelic.
Famous beyond all other hymns of Irish origin was his Latin hymn,
the ¢ Altus prositor,” composed in Tona in the latter half of the sixth
century. This hymn was an early ‘‘ Paradise Lost’”” and * Paradise
Regained,”’ for it told of the creation of the angels, the world, and all
creatures; the fall of Satan, and the redemption of man. Like his
predecessors in poetry in the North, exile bore hard upon him, for he
had a passionate love of country. Through the centuries which fol-
lowed, flashes of poetry fitfully lighted up the twilight past. Ulster
poetic art stood high at the last Conference or Contention of the
Bards in the seventeenth century; henceforth, for a time, 1ts songs
echoed only in retired glens, in lonely woods, or over the bare mountain
heather. When the tumult of war subsided, the Plantation of Ulster
had taken place. [t was a common error to suppose that the new
settlers were alien in race. They were mostly Scots and mostly Gaels;
their ancestors had gone to Alba or Caledonia as conquerors of old
time. There had bheen intimate relations between the sea-divided from
time immemorial, and they were bound by the three-fold bond of a
common langwage, literature, and music. They had often fought side
by side in war, and the English Parliament recognised their friendship
by prohibiting marriage between the Irish and Scots. Subsequently
the Jacobite wars broughtt them closely together. Iven the English-
speaking settlers must have been largely Jacobite, for nothing used to
be so popular in Ulster as songs about ‘‘ Bonnie Prince Charlie?’ and
the ‘‘ Young Chevalier.”” ¢ The Blackbird’”’ was also an Irish-Faglish
song on tthe same theme. There was still sung in the Nonth, in Irish,
in the glens, the laments for * Seurlas.”” They loved and cherished
the exquisite music of the Gael, and, thercfore, could not have perse-
cuted the native performers. The bards had lost their chiefs, but tho
harpers won new patrons. The last great gathering of Trish harpers

in Belfast in July, 1792, was organised by Dr. James M’Donnell, whose
immediate predecessors had come over at the time of the plantation,
and whose great grandfather led the Irish contingent under Montroso

in Scotland. At this gathering Edward Bunting was engaged to take

down the music, and many Irish songs were gathered afiterwards, so
that Ireland owed to these Ulstermen the preservation of her greatest
musical treasure. Dr. Drennan was born in Belfast in 1754, and died
in 1820. Irom his time onward there had been a continuous golden
chain of Ulster poets, including Conolly, Walsh, Ferguson, Browne,
Duffy, O’Hagan, Irwin, Allingham, Ingram, Brooke, and MacAleese.
It was remarkable that the gift of song flashed from all classes. The
weavers were particularly vocal. It was impossible to enumerate all
writers, but the mame of Stephen Gwynn, born in Donegal, had been
welcome everywhere, and that of ‘“ A.E.,”” George Russell, of Lurgan
birth, was recognised as the high chief of melodions mysticism.
Supreme over all was the name of Samuel Ferguson. This Ulsterman
was the first to render the true spirit of Irish song into an English
form. He was the first to recast in mighty mould examples of the
ancient heroic literature of our land with eminent success, because he,
t0, shared the living fire of the heroic mind.

“ THE FREEMAN’S JOURNALL.”

. The celebration of the 150th anmiversary of the foundation of any
newspaper i1s an event of rare occurrence, and in the history of Irish
periodical literature it has only happened once before.  Therefore,
when an organ of world-wide fame like the *‘IFreeman’s Journal
attains that super-eminence the fact deserves the widest recognition
in literary circles. To mark the occasion the grand old journal has

recently issued a third Jubilee Number, a triumph of latter day pro- |

duction, containing special amticles of move than passing interest,
dealing with its long and historic career. Mr. T. Wyse Power, a
former member of the staff, contributes a brilliant history of the paper,
which gained the prize of £75 offered by the proprietors, and Mr.
Thos. Markham, for his able and exhaustive memoir of Charles Lucas,
its founder, obtaing a prize of £50.

and ever voiced, with no uncertain sound, the national aspitations of
the Irish people, and proved a strong bulwark of defence in times of
oppression. From the days of Charles Lucas and Hemry Brooke, ‘‘the
unpurchaseable Harvey ‘‘and the younger Grattan, through the Grays,
father and som, down to our times, it has been a fearless advocate for
the right, and its proprietors have endured persecution and punish-
ment for the faith that was in them. In its columns are enshrined
the sufferings of her sons in the vicissitudes of their country, during
the long space of a century and a half—years of apparent failure, set-
back and despair—the rise and fall of a nation, in due time to be suc-
coeded by hearty, hopeful labour and eventual triumph. Through it
all the “ Freeman’’ bore aloft the banmer of progress in the vanguard
of the warriors battling in their ¢ountry’s cause, and to-day, crowned
with the wisdom of age, yet endowed with the glory of youth, it stands
forth the emblem of our land that no hostile power could crush. Ad
multos annos!

E. R. McC. DIX.
WAYFARERS.
Take up the task anew—the night is fialling,
Weaving the shadows of her own despair;
The still sad voice across the twilight calling,
Making a mock of life, of death a snare.
Mall-marked with failure; why this bitter striving,
This useless zeal that mocks the futile goal;
This fever heat of passion unforgiving
Where death has cast her shackles on the soul?

Oh march breast-forward; snap the galling traces;
Blast you the locks and burst the prison bans;
With bleeding footsteps on the stoney spaces,
With shining faces fixed upon the stars!
RANDAL McDONNELL.

overlooked or unidentifiod by his first editor, Thomas Wallis. Messrs.
M. H. Gill will be the publishers.

grace of the Speaker. FLEET STREET.
